vocabulary.definition: The average distance of each data value from the mean of the data set
vocabulary.visual: Data: 8, 10, 12. Mean = 10. Distances from mean: 2, 0, 2. MAD = (2+0+2) ÷ 3 = 1.33

vocabulary.definition: The difference between a data value and the mean
vocabulary.visual: If mean = 20 and value = 17, deviation = 17 − 20 = −3 (3 below the mean)

vocabulary.definition: The distance a number is from zero, always positive
vocabulary.visual: |−3| = 3 and |3| = 3 — both are 3 units from zero

vocabulary.definition: How far apart the values in a data set are from each other and from the center
vocabulary.visual: Low spread: 8, 9, 10, 11 (close together). High spread: 2, 9, 10, 25 (far apart)

vocabulary.definition: The shape and spread of a data set
vocabulary.visual: A set clustered tightly around the mean has low MAD; a set spread far from the mean has high MAD

vocabulary.definition: How spread out the values in a data set are
vocabulary.visual: Low variability (MAD = 1): very consistent. High variability (MAD = 8): very spread out

launch.narrative: Coach wants to know which basketball player is more consistent: Player A scored 18, 22, 20, 24, 16 points in the last 5 games. Player B scored 10, 30, 25, 12, 23 points. Both players average 20 points per game. Which player would you count on to score close to 20 every night?
launch.contextImage: Two player stat cards side by side with game-by-game scoring
noticePrompts: Both players have the same mean. What is different about their scores?
Which player's scores stay closer to 20?
Which player has the biggest single-game difference from 20?
wonderPrompts: How can we measure how spread out each player's scores are?
Is there a number that tells us which player is more consistent?

explore.label: Calculate the Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) for Player A's scores: 18, 22, 20, 24, 16. Follow each step.

summaryRow.label: MAD = Sum of Absolute Deviations ÷ Number of Values

discourse.prompt: Player A's MAD is 2.4 points. If Player B's MAD is 7.2 points, what does that tell you about their consistency?
discourse.sentenceFrame: Player A has a ___ MAD, which means their scores are ___ to the mean. Player B has a ___ MAD, which means their scores are ___ from the mean. The more consistent player is ___ because ___.

optional.stem: The data 5, 7, 9 has mean 7. What is the mean absolute deviation (MAD)?
What does a larger MAD tell you about a data set?
choices: The values are more spread out from the mean
optional.explanation: MAD measures average distance from the mean; larger MAD means more spread.
Every value equals the mean, so each distance is 0 and MAD = 0.

optional.stem: For the data 2, 4, 6 (mean 4), what is the MAD?

approaching.stem: The mean of a data set is 15. One value is 11. What is the absolute deviation of that value from the mean?
A data set has absolute deviations of 3, 1, 5, 2, 4. What is the MAD?
The mean of a data set is 20. A value is 26. What is the absolute deviation?
Team A has MAD = 2.1 points. Team B has MAD = 6.8 points. Which team is more consistent?
choices: Team A — lower MAD means scores are closer to the mean
Team B — higher MAD means better performance
approaching.explanation: Lower MAD means less spread — Team A's scores stay closer to their average, making them more consistent.

approaching.instructions: The mean is 10. Place each data value on the number line, then find how far each is from the mean: Data: 7, 10, 13.
hints: 7 is 3 units below the mean. 10 is exactly at the mean. 13 is 3 units above the mean.

onLevel.label: Match each MAD value to what it tells you about the data.

onLevel.instructions: Sort these data sets from LEAST spread out (lowest MAD) to MOST spread out (highest MAD).
hints: Lower MAD = less spread. Higher MAD = more spread.
Set A has values very close to 10. Set C has values far from 10.
onLevel.label: Rank from least to most spread out.

onLevel.stem: Two runners have the same average time of 60 seconds. Runner A's MAD is 1.2 seconds. Runner B's MAD is 5.8 seconds. Which runner is the coach more likely to pick for a relay race that needs a reliable time?
choices: Runner A — lower MAD means more consistent times
Runner B — higher MAD means faster potential
Either — they have the same average
Neither — MAD doesn't matter for relay races
onLevel.explanation: Runner A's MAD of 1.2 means times are usually within 1.2 seconds of 60. Runner B's times vary more widely. For reliability, pick Runner A.

extending.title: Find the MAD Error
extending.correctWork: You must use absolute values of the deviations: |−10| = 10, |−5| = 5, |0| = 0, |5| = 5, |10| = 10. MAD = (10 + 5 + 0 + 5 + 10) ÷ 5 = 30 ÷ 5 = 6.
hints: MAD stands for Mean ABSOLUTE Deviation. What does 'absolute' mean you need to do?
Take the absolute value of each deviation before adding them: |−10| = 10, |−5| = 5, etc.

extending.prompt: Two basketball teams both average 75 points per game. Team A's last 5 scores: 73, 76, 74, 77, 75. Team B's last 5 scores: 60, 90, 65, 85, 75. Calculate the MAD for each team and explain which team a coach would prefer if they need predictable scoring.
extending.sentenceFrame: Team A's MAD = ___. Team B's MAD = ___. Team A is ___ because ___. A coach would prefer Team ___ for predictable scoring because ___.

headers: Would you want LOW or HIGH MAD?

rows.scenario: A factory making bolts that must be exactly 5 cm long
rows.why: Bolts need to be consistent — close to 5 cm every time

rows.scenario: A game show where contestants try to score as many different point values as possible
rows.why: Variety in scores is the goal — spread is good here

rows.scenario: A bus schedule where arrival times should be reliable
rows.why: Passengers need buses to arrive consistently on time

extending.label: For each scenario, would you want low or high MAD? Explain.

connect.scenario: Two soccer goalkeepers track goals allowed per game over 6 games. Keeper A: 1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1 (mean = 1.67, MAD ≈ 0.56). Keeper B: 0, 4, 1, 3, 0, 2 (mean = 1.67, MAD ≈ 1.22). The coach needs to pick a starter for the championship game.
connect.promptQuestion: Both keepers allow the same average goals. Which should the coach pick and why?
connect.prompt: Both keepers have a mean of ___. Keeper A's MAD is ___, which means ___. Keeper B's MAD is ___, which means ___. The coach should pick ___ because ___.

exitTicket.stem: Data set: 4, 8, 6, 10, 2. The mean is 6. What is the MAD?
